coredata.dc:title: Targeted antibacterial potency against multidrug resistance pathogen enhanced with N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots selectively recognizes rifampicin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Raut, Jiko
Patra, Dipanwita
Mandal, Shanti M.
Mandal, Sukhendu
Sahoo, Prithidipa
coredata.dc:description: A simple environment-friendly nitrogen, sulphur dual doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) has been developed via a simple microwave-assisted method using mercaptosuccinic acid and diethylenetriamine. The interactions of the N, S-CQDs with various randomly chosen common commercial antibiotics were studied using their indigenous fluorescence properties. The N, S-CQDs were found to selectively interact with rifampicin over other tested antibiotics via the interaction between the accessible functional groups of N, S-CQDs and rifampicin resulting in loss of its fluorescence. The interaction of N, S-CQDs and rifampicin was tight and irreversible. Thus, the environmental exposure of free rifampicin, rifampicin-directed antimicrobial resistance among bacteria, and rifampicin toxicity might be mitigated in the future by the use of these N, S-CQDs. While tested on the HuH-7 human cell line, we found that the N, S-CQDs neither can kill nor impair the proliferation of the cells, indicating its non-cytotoxic nature. Furthermore, the quantum dot also possesses antimicrobial activity against both Gram-positive, and Gram-negative bacteria and their drug resistance varieties possibly by disrupting the cellular boundaries. N, S-CQDs could be effective next-generation antimicrobials for having synergistic nature when applied with other common antibiotics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N, S dual doped carbon quantum dots
Multidrug resistance pathogens
Selectively binds rifampicin
Synergistic effects
Antibacterial potency

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of rifampicin in live cell
N, S-CQDs inhibit the growth of drug resistance pathogens
N, S-CQDs works synergistically with existing antibiotics to kill bacterial pathogens

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: P.S. acknowledges SERB, India, for awarding her SERB power grant [Project file no. Ref. No. SPG/2020/000713]. Authors sincerely acknowledge the Department of Chemistry Visva-Bharati, Santiniketan, Department of Microbiology, University of Calcutta, India, and Central Research Facility, IIT Kharagpur, India for providing infrastructural facilities. The authors also acknowledge A Karar and Prof. AK Mahapatra, IIEST, Shibpur, for helping with the data collection from TCSPC.

P.S. acknowledges SERB, India, for awarding her SERB power grant [Project file no. Ref. No. SPG/2020/000713]. Authors sincerely acknowledge the Department of Chemistry Visva-Bharati, Santiniketan, Department of Microbiology, University of Calcutta, India, and Central Research Facility, IIT Kharagpur, India for providing infrastructural facilities. The authors also acknowledge A Karar and Prof. AK Mahapatra, IIEST, Shibpur, for helping with the data collection from TCSPC.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image (Inset: hydrodynamic diameter measured by DLS) (B) FT-IR spectra and (C) EDX data of N, S-CQDs.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of N, S-CQDs (B) Fluorescence emission spectrum of the N, S-CQDs. (C) Emission spectra of N, S-CQDs at Different excitation. (D) pH response measurement of N, S-CQDs.
(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N, S-CQDs in presence of various concentrations of RIF. (B) competitive fluorescence spectra of N, S-CQDs with different antibiotics at 440 nm (λex = 360 nm).

(A) second-order polynomial fitting of Stern-Volmer plot for RIF (B) Plot of Kapp Vs concentration of RIF (C) Excitation spectra (red gradient) & emission (blue line) of N, S-CQDs, absorption spectra of N, S-CQDs (violet line), RIF (orange line) & N, S-CQDs + RIF (magenta gradient).

Fluorescence microscopic images of HuH-7 cells treated with N, S-CQDs and RIF (a) Bright filed at 10X after treatment with 40 µg/mL N, S-CQDs (b) Fluorescence field at 10X after treatment with 40 µg/mL N, S-CQDs (C) Bright filed at 10X after treatment with 40 µg/mL N, S-CQDs and rifampicin 40 µg/mL (d) Fluorescence field at 10X after treatment with 40 µg/mL N, S-CQDs and rifampicin 40 µg/mL. All images are acquired with applied wavelength λex = 360 nm, λem = 440 nm filter used DAPI.

Fluorescence microscopic images of E. coli MTCC1687 cells treated with N, S-CQDs and RIF (a) Bright filed at 100X after treatment with 40 µg/mL N, S-CQDs (b) Fluorescence field at 100X after treatment with 40 µg/mL N, S-CQDs (c) Bright filed at 100X after treatment with 40 µg/mL N, S-CQDs and rifampicin 40 µg/mL (d) Fluorescence field at 100X after treatment with 40 µg/mL N, S-CQDs and rifampicin 40 µg/mL. All images are acquired with applied wavelength λex = 360 nm, λem = 440 nm filter used DAPI.
MIC of different compounds against various bacteria.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *Rif, Chl, Str, Amp, and Van represent rifampicin, chloramphenicol, streptomycin, ampicillin, and vancomycin, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synergy of N, S-CQDs along with different antibiotics against S. aureus MTCC96 by checkerboard assay.

head.ce:title: Targeted antibacterial potency against multidrug resistance pathogen enhanced with N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots selectively recognizes rifampicin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Visva-Bharati University, Santiniketan 731235, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Visva-Bharati University, Santiniketan 731235, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microbiology, University of Calcutta, Kolkata 700019, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microbiology, University of Calcutta, Kolkata 700019, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Central Research Facility, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Central Research Facility, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple environment-friendly nitrogen, sulphur dual doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) has been developed via a simple microwave-assisted method using mercaptosuccinic acid and diethylenetriamine. The interactions of the N, S-CQDs with various randomly chosen common commercial antibiotics were studied using their indigenous fluorescence properties. The N, S-CQDs were found to selectively interact with rifampicin over other tested antibiotics via the interaction between the accessible functional groups of N, S-CQDs and rifampicin resulting in loss of its fluorescence. The interaction of N, S-CQDs and rifampicin was tight and irreversible. Thus, the environmental exposure of free rifampicin, rifampicin-directed antimicrobial resistance among bacteria, and rifampicin toxicity might be mitigated in the future by the use of these N, S-CQDs. While tested on the HuH-7 human cell line, we found that the N, S-CQDs neither can kill nor impair the proliferation of the cells, indicating its non-cytotoxic nature. Furthermore, the quantum dot also possesses antimicrobial activity against both Gram-positive, and Gram-negative bacteria and their drug resistance varieties possibly by disrupting the cellular boundaries. N, S-CQDs could be effective next-generation antimicrobials for having synergistic nature when applied with other common antibiotics.

ce:keyword.ce:text: N, S dual doped carbon quantum dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: I have shared the link to my data/code at the attach file steps

ce:section.ce:para: Rifampicin (RIF) is one of the most significant and often used antibiotics for tuberculosis (TB) and to treat infections pertaining to prosthetic joints, meningitis, and osteomyelitis [1,2]. Overuse of RIF can enhance the drug resistance of pathogenic bacteria in the body, making it more challenging to eradicate the bacteria from the body and treat illnesses [3]. Additionally, resistance might develop spontaneously as a result of target modification as well as the active efflux of antibiotics from the cell [4,5]. The development of resistant bacteria is also aided by antibiotics that have been improperly given in sub-therapeutic and sub-inhibitory concentration through genetic modifications like altered gene expression, mutagenesis and horizontal gene transfer (HGT) [6].

Over the past few decades, antibiotic overuse has also exerted a negative impact on the environment and world health [7]. Numerous factors, such as the release of antibiotics into the water stream by animals and humans, might be blamed for the significant presence of antibiotics in the environment and the rise of AMRs (Antimicrobial resistance). If the antibiotics are not been removed or destroyed from the environment, they will eventually find their way into drinking water, groundwater, and surface water [8]. Therefore, to protect the environment and human health, we must minimize antibiotic exposure and stop the development of antibiotic resistance.

Carbon Quantum dots (CQDs) are unique nanomaterial with strong and controllable fluorescence properties. Due to their great photostability, flexible emission wavelength, low toxicity and strong water solubility, CQDs have been used extensively as fluorescent nanoprobes to quantitatively and selectively detect numerous substances that were previously identified by fluorescent dyes [9]. Herein, we developed a simple environmentally friendly nitrogen, sulphur dual doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) via a simple microwave-assisted method using mercaptosuccinic acid (MSA) and diethylenetriamine (DETA) as nitrogen and sulphur sources, respectively. The interaction of the N, S-CQDs with RIF and other various antibiotic molecules was studied using its indigenous fluorescence properties and it was found that N, S-CQDs will abolish the RIF-mediated killing of the tested bacterial pathogen and also showed a synergistic relationship with other antibiotics. Also, we've discovered that N, S-CQDs prevent the development of both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. And thereby, N, S-CQDS is highly beneficial to prevent RIF-mediated antibiotic resistance as well as to suppress bacterial growth. The binding studies of N, S-CQDs are established by different spectroscopic methods after interaction with antibiotics. Its cytotoxicity towards both the human cell line and bacteria was tested.

Mercaptosuccinic acid and Diethylenetriamine (DETA) are purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Pvt. Ltd. (India). Rifampicin and all other antibiotics are purchased from TCI India. Here double distilled (dd) water is used as the solvent.

Transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM JEOL JEM 2100) was used to understand the morphology of the N, S-CQDs. DLS data were recorded on a Malvern 4800 Autosizer with a 7132 correlator. Fourier transform Infrared (FT-IR) data were collected on a Nexus TM 870 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Hitachi Model F-7100 Fluorescence spectrophotometer. UV spectral measurements were performed on a Hitachi U-2910 spectrophotometer. Multifunctional time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) fluorescence spectrophotometer (Model 1057, Fluorlog, Horiba Scientific Tech. USA) was used for this experiment. Centrifuged are done by Benchmark-Hermle Z216 MK Refrigerated Benchtop Centrifuge. Live cell images were taken in an Olympus IX73 fluorescence microscope.

Nitrogen, sulphur dual doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) were synthesized by the microwave-assisted method. Typically, 0.2 g of mercaptosuccinic acid was dissolved in 40 mL of double distilled water in a 100 mL Erlenmeyer flask and stirred with a magnetic stirrer. Once a clear solution was achieved, 1 mL of DETA was added and stirred for 3–5 h. The solution mixture was thereafter heated for 4 min using a standard microwave oven (560 W). After that, allow the solution to cool before centrifuging it for 15 min at 10,000 rpm and then purifying it with a 0.22 µm membrane filter before storing it at 4 °C for further characterization and application.

In PBS buffer (pH = 7.4), the presence of RIF was determined. 1 mL of N, S-CQDs and 1 mL PBS buffer was added to a quartz cuvette. After that, various RIF concentrations were added from a 10−4 M stock solution, and the fluorescence spectrum was captured using a 360 nm excitation.

The quantum yield (QY) of N, S-CQDs, measured at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, was calculated using the following equation [10], using quinine sulphate (QY = 0.54) as the reference standard.
(1)
Q x
=
Q
st
I x
/
I
st
A
st
/
A x
n x 2
/
n
st
2

Where Q denotes the quantum yield of N, S-CQDs, n is the refractive index, I is the observed integrated emission intensity, and A is the absorbance at 360 nm. “st” refers to the standard with known QY and “x” for the sample. To reduce the reabsorption effects, the absorbance value of each solution was kept below 0.05 at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

The bacterial strains (Staphylococcus aureus MTCC96, MDR Enterococcus sp. 291, MDR Pseudomonas aeruginosa MV36846, Escherichia coli MTCC1687) were grown in Mueller-Hinton (MH) broth medium at 37℃ and used for the imaging experiment and assessment of the antimicrobial properties of N, S-CQDs.
A brief discussion of MIC of different compounds against various bacteria is provided in the Supporting Information.
The synergistic or antagonistic effect of the N, S-CQDs with commonly used antibiotics was analyzed through the checkerboard method (See Supporting Information).

The intracellular protein leakage has been estimated by measuring the proteins external to the cell upon treatment with N, S-CQDs. Leakage of intracellular protein would be the reflection of damage of cellular compartments. The procedure has been detailed in supporting information.
Details of the bacterial cell imaging study in S. aureus. with N, S-CQDs and rifampicin is provided in Supporting Information.
Details procedure of cell culture (Huh-7) is supplied in the Supporting Information.
An MTT test was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of compound N, S-CQDs in accordance with a standard protocol [11] (See Supporting Information).
The details of the fluorescence imaging study in animal cell lines (HuH-7) with N, S-CQDs and rifampicin in PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) is provided in the Supporting Information.

To understand the morphology of the as-prepared N, S-CQDs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were carried out. Fig. 1 A, clearly demonstrates the formation of spherical and well-dispersed N, S-CQDs with an average particle size of 1 nm (inset in Fig. 1A). HRTEM image (Fig. S1) further demonstrated the crystalline nature of the N, S-CQDs.

FT-IR spectra were used to characterize the surface functional group of the prepared N, S-CQDs (Fig. 1B). The peaks at 3423 cm−1 and 2939 cm−1, respectively, are caused by the stretching vibrations of –OH/–NH and C-H. Peaks at 1571 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 represent the bending vibration of N-H and C = O of amide. The characteristic peak at 1488 cm−1 corresponds to C-N. The two other peaks at 1392 cm−1 and 1313 cm−1 are stretching vibrations of C-N which confirms the nitrogen doping. Peaks at 982 cm−1, 824 cm−1 and 667 cm−1 are associated with the bending vibration of N-H and C-H and bands at 2137 cm−1 and 1116 cm−1 are assigned to S-H and C-S stretching, implying Sulphur doping [12–17].
Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was used to analyze the elemental composition of N, S-CQDs and revealed the existence of C, N, O and S atoms (Fig. 1C).

The optical properties of N, S-CQDs were studied by analyzing UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. Fig. 2 A displays a prominent absorption peak at 324 nm caused by the n-π* transition of the -C-N and C = O/C = N bonds with a shoulder peak appearing at 478 nm [18–20]. Additionally, N, S-CQDs exhibit a strong blue fluorescence under UV light (365 nm) (inset in Fig. 2A).

N, S-CQDs exhibit a very strong emission peak at 440 nm upon excitation at 360 nm (Fig. 2B). Furthermore, Fig. 2C exhibited that fluorescence intensity increases with increasing excitation wavelength from 324 nm to 360 nm and after that again intensity decreases from 370 to 400 nm excitation. Fluorescence emission intensity is maximum at 440 nm when excited at 360 nm. With increasing excitation, emission wavelength red-shifted (Fig. 2C). The excitation-dependent fluorescence activity is due to the surface defects of N, S-CQDs. The prepared N, S-CQDs QY was found to be 44.5%. Additionally, the stability of N, S-CQDs was examined at various pH levels. In the pH range of 7 to 8 (Fig. 2D), the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs was found to be very stable and does not change. As a result, PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) is used during the entire experiment to maintain physiological pH.

By mixing various concentrations of RIF into the N, S-CQDs solution and measuring the intensity of the fluorescence emission, the potential of N, S-CQDs as a fluorescent probe for RIF was ascertained. Fig. 3 A shows that as the concentration of RIF rises from 10 µl to 900 µl, the intensity of the N, S-CQDs fluorescence continuously declines, demonstrating that RIF can successfully quench the N, S-CQDs fluorescence. Using regression analysis, the binding affinity of the N, S-CQDs for the RIF was determined to be 2.43×105 M−1 (Fig. S2). The limit of detection was estimated to be 77 nM using the formula 3σ/m (where σ is the standard deviation and m is the slope of the curve) (Fig. S3) (Table S1).

By analyzing fluorescence changes in the presence of various antibiotics (gentamycin, doxycycline, chloramphenicol, streptomycin, ampicillin, vancomycin, neomycin, norfloxacin, amikacin, Levofloxacin, Streptomycin, tobramycin etc.) the selectivity of N, S-CQDs for RIF was further determined. A quartz cuvette was filled with 1 mL of N, S-CQDs and 1 mL of PBS buffer (pH = 7.4). After that, 10−4 M stock solutions of each antibiotic were added and the fluorescence spectra were measured while being excited at 360 nm. As seen in (Fig. 3B) only rifampicin can completely quench the fluorescence of the N, S-CQDs while the other antibiotic has no such impact on the fluorescence of the N, S-CQDs. Therefore, N, S-CQDs were employed to specifically detect RIF.

The fluorescence of N, S-CQDs can be quenched in a number of ways, including static quenching, dynamic quenching or combined dynamic and static quenching. The fluorescence quenching mechanism can be described using Stern-Volmer equation [21] and the data can be understood by graphing F0/F vs [Q]. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of N, S-CQDs in the absence and presence of RIF (quencher) and [Q] is quencher (RIF) concentration. Depending on the quenching mechanism, both nonlinear and linear Stern-Volmer plots are possible.

For dynamic quenching the Stern-Volmer equation is –
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K D
Q

KD is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, KD = kqτ0, where kq stands for the bimolecular quenching constant and τ0 is the lifetime of the fluorescence molecules in the absence of a quencher. By plotting, F0/F Vs [Q] will get a linear graph, which is not observed in our case.

For static quenching, fluorescence quenching is occurred by forming a ground state complex which is not fluorescent. For this quenching the Stern-Volmer equation is –
(3)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K S
Q
Here, KS is the association constant. Here also by plotting F0/F Vs [Q] we get a linear graph.

In case of combined dynamic & static quenching the Stern-Volmer plot is –
(4)
F 0
/ F =
1
+
K D
Q
1
+
K S
Q
The plot of F0/F Vs [Q] gives a positive upward curve, which is seen in our case (Fig. 4 A).

There is another type of quenching, which is referred as the “sphere of action”. When the quencher is closed to the fluorophore at the time of excitation and quenches the fluorescence without generating a ground state complex then this type of static quenching is denoted as “sphere of action”. The Stern-Volmer equation for this type of quenching is –
(5)
F 0
/ F =
1 +
K D
Q
e
V [ Q ]
Here, V is the volume of the sphere. plotting F0/F Vs [Q] also gives a positive upward curve, like a combined dynamic & static quenching process.

So, we could eliminate the idea of static quenching and dynamic quenching as in our case the plot of F0/F Vs [Q] gives a positive upward curve (Fig. 4A). However, an upward positive curve is produced by both cases, combined dynamic & static quenching and sphere of action. In the case of combined dynamic & static quenching fluorescence is quenched simultaneously by two mechanisms, one is dynamic quenching and second is static quenching by forming a ground state nonfluorescent complex. Now to confirm the involvement of dynamic quenching Time-resolved fluorescent spectroscopy was done. Fig. S4 & Table S2 reveal that the average lifetime of the N, S-CQDs was nearly unchanged after the addition of RIF which denies the involvement of dynamic quenching. Furthermore, to verify the involvement of static quenching by ground state complex formation UV–vis spectroscopic measurement was done. When ground state complex forms the absorption spectra of N, S-CQDs are expected to be changed but it was seen that there was no shift in the peak after the addition of RIF (Fig. S5) which reject the idea of the involvement of static quenching by the formation of ground state complex. Additionally, the involvement of ground state complex formation in the quenching mechanism was also confirmed by following the modified Stern-Volmer equation-.
(6)
K
app
=
F 0
/ F - - 1
/
Q
=
K S
+
K D
+
K S
K D
Q

By plotting (F0/F – 1)/[Q]) Vs [Q] if a straight line is obtained then we could say that the involvement of quenching is by the formation of a ground state complex, which is not seen here. In our case we see a deviation from the straight line (Fig. 4B). So, the idea of combined dynamic & static quenching is also rejected.

Now, as we see from Stern-Volmer plot of F0/F Vs [Q] an upward positive curve was obtained but combined dynamic & static quenching was not involved, so we can predict that the “sphere of action” is the reason for quenching of the N, S-CQDs [22].

Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 4C, the absorption spectra of N, S-CQDs with RIF overlapped with the excitation & emission spectra of N, S-CQDs without RIF. Thus, the Inner Filter Effect (IFE) is responsible for the quenching of N, S-CQDs. Therefore, we may conclude that the fluorescence quenching of N, S-CQDs by RIF is caused by the Sphere of action and inner filter effect (IFE) [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of rifampicin in live cell
N, S-CQDs inhibit the growth of drug resistance pathogens
ce:section.ce:para: As the N, S-CQDs bind with RIF, we anticipated that the N, S-CQDs will abolish the RIF-mediated killing of the tested bacterial pathogen. Strikingly, it has been found that the N, S-CQDs also show anti-bacterial activity on the tested organisms (Table 1 ). We have found that N, S-CQDs inhibit the growth of both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria (Supplementary Figs. S7 and S8). Moreover, it also impaired the growth of the multi-drug resistant Enterococcus sp. and Pseudomonas sp. (Table 1). The N, S-CQDs kills the bacterial cell by disrupting the cell boundaries as evident from the cellular protein leakage experiment (Supplementary Fig. S9) and scanning electron micrograph of the treated cells also showed ruptured cell boundaries. It has also been found that the N, S-CQDs indeed can rescue the bacterial cell from RIF-mediated killing. The MIC value of RIF failed to kill the tested bacterium in presence of N, S-CQDs. (Table S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: N, S-CQDs works synergistically with existing antibiotics to kill bacterial pathogens
ce:section.ce:para: The antimicrobial potency of the N, S-CQDs were further been explored for its future use in combinatorial therapy. It has been found that the QD showed an antagonistic effect with RIF (Table S3) while applied to S. aureus. However, other tested common antibiotics like chloramphenicol, streptomycin, ampicillin, vancomycin showed a synergistic relationship with the N, S-CQDs when applied to the test pathogen (Table 2 ).

In summary, we designed an N, S dual doped carbon quantum dots that selectively detects RIF by a fluorescence quenching mechanism and has strong water solubility, high quantum yield (QY = 44.5%), and excellent biocompatibility. Moreover, N, S-CQDs are very environmentally benign because it doesn’t require the use of organic solvents. Without causing any cytotoxicity to the test samples, N, S-CQDs may also be employed to identify RIF within the physiological system of bacterial and mammalian cells. These N, S-CQDs have potent and targeted antibacterial properties without ostensibly leading to drug resistance. The nanoprobe detects rifampicin selectively over other antibiotics via interaction between the surface functional groups of N, S-CQDs and rifampicin which caused the fluorescence quenching. The created nanoprobe was then employed to eradicate bacterial pathogens. Thus, we could reduce the exposure effect of rifampicin by blocking its active site with our nanoprobe, so it could not affect our environment and reduced the chance of being antibiotic-resistant.

Jiko Raut: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Dipanwita Patra: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Shanti M. Mandal: Resources. Sukhendu Mandal: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Conceptualization. Prithidipa Sahoo: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Investigation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: P.S. acknowledges SERB, India, for awarding her SERB power grant [Project file no. Ref. No. SPG/2020/000713]. Authors sincerely acknowledge the Department of Chemistry Visva-Bharati, Santiniketan, Department of Microbiology, University of Calcutta, India, and Central Research Facility, IIT Kharagpur, India for providing infrastructural facilities. The authors also acknowledge A Karar and Prof. AK Mahapatra, IIEST, Shibpur, for helping with the data collection from TCSPC.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.114761.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanistic Principles Behind Molecular Mechanism of Rifampicin Resistance in Mutant RNA Polymerase Beta Subunit of Mycobacterium tuberculosis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Singh, S. Grover, S. Sinha, M. Das, P. Somvanshi, and A. Grover, Mechanistic Principles Behind Molecular Mechanism of Rifampicin Resistance in Mutant RNA Polymerase Beta Subunit of Mycobacterium tuberculosis. J Cell Biochem., 2017,118, 4594, 10.1002/jcb.26124.
H. S. Fraimow, Systemic antimicrobial therapy in osteomyelitis. Semin Plast Surg., 2009, 23(2), 90–99, 10.1055/s-0029-1214161.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Diagnostic Accuracy of GeneXpert MTB/RIF Assay in Comparison to Conventional Drug Susceptibility Testing Method for the Diagnosis of Multidrug-Resistant Tuberculosis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Pandey, N. D. Pant, K. R. Rijal, B. Shrestha, S. Kattel, M. R. Banjara, B. Maharjan and K. C. Rajendra, Diagnostic Accuracy of GeneXpert MTB/RIF Assay in Comparison to Conventional Drug Susceptibility Testing Method for the Diagnosis of Multidrug-Resistant Tuberculosis. Plos One, 2017, 12, 1-6, 10.1371/journal.pone.0169798.
A. F. Read, R. J. Woods, Antibiotic resistance management. Evol Med Public Health., 2014, 2014(1), 147, 10.1093/emph/eou024.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multidrug resistance pumps in bacteria: variations on a theme

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Lewis, Multidrug resistance pumps in bacteria: variations on a theme. Trends Biochem. Sci., 1994, 19, 119–23, 10.1016/0968-0004(94)90204-6.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Off-label abuse of antibiotics by bacteria

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. K. Viswanathan, Off-label abuse of antibiotics by bacteria. Gut Microbes., 2014, 5(1), 3-4, 10.4161/gmic.28027.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: WHO's 2013 global report on tuberculosis: successes, threats, and opportunities

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Zumla, A. George, V. Sharma, N. Herbert and B. Masham, WHO's 2013 global report on tuberculosis: successes, threats, and opportunities. Lancet, 2013, 382, 1765-1767, 10.1016/S0140-6736(13)62078-4.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effluent from drug manufactures contains extremely high levels of pharmaceuticals

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. G. J. Larsson, C. de Pedro, and N. Paxeus, Effluent from drug manufactures contains extremely high levels of pharmaceuticals. J. Hazard. Mater., 2007, 148(3), 751–755, 10.1016/j.jhazmat.2007.07.008.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ionic liquid assisted electrospinning of quantum dots/elastomer composite nanofibers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhu, S. Wei, R. Patil, D. Rutman, A.S. Kucknoor, A. Wang, Z. Guo, Ionic liquid assisted electrospinning of quantum dots/elastomer composite nanofibers. Polymer, 2011, 52(9), 1954–1962, 10.1016/j.polymer.2011.02.051.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of carbon nitride quantum dots as a highly selective and sensitive fluorescent sensor for the tetracycline detection

Biosens Bioelectron., 2015, 68, 210-217, 10.1016/j.bios.2014.12.024.
